title: LifeGPT: topology-agnostic generative pretrained transformer model for cellular automata
abstract: Conway's Game of Life (Life), a famous cellular automata (CA) algorithm, exhibits complex, initial condition-sensitive dynamics. Modeling Life without knowing the system's topology presents a challenge, motivating the development of algorithms capable of generalizing across grid configurations and boundary conditions. We introduce LifeGPT, a decoder-only generative pretrained transformer (GPT) model with rotary positional embedding (RoPE) and forgetful causal masking (FCM), capable of computing a single-timestep global state transition in Life on a toroidal grid without prior knowledge of grid size or boundary conditions. LifeGPT is topology-agnostic, achieving near-perfect accuracy in capturing the deterministic rules of Life given sufficiently diverse training data. We show recursive simulation of Life is possible using LifeGPT within an autoregressive loop. LifeGPT can also be trained on grids of varying sizes while retaining near-perfect accuracy. Finally, we propose future research, like using LifeGPT-like models to infer CA rulesets from real-world data, which could advance predictive modeling.
results: Details on the implementation of the model, training, and datasets used are provided in Materials and Methods (Materials and methods). LifeGPT is architected as a decoder-only transformer model with causally-masked self attention—in addition to FCM during training—which utilized 12 transformer layers and 8 attention heads to capture complex patterns in Life. The model's maximum sequence length was configured to accommodate a data corresponding to ICs and NGSs. Rotary positional embedding (RoPE) was used to maintain spatial awareness, while the Adam optimizer and crossentropy loss (CEL) function guided the training process (see "Model architecture and hardware information" for a full list of hyperparameters). The best-performing version of LifeGPT was trained on training data with broad-entropy ICs, though using high-entropy ICs was also tested (see "Training set generation" and "Training data ordering effects"). Our final model displayed rapid convergence to a range of crossentropy loss (CEL) values from ~0.4 to 0.2 (Fig. 2A). We suspect the reason the loss stays far greater than 0 is that there is no causal relationship between earlier tokens and later tokens in ICs within the training data, since ICs were generated stochastically (see "Training set generation"). Since FCM was the only masking strategy used (see section 4.4 for details on implementation and Supplementary section 4.4 for brief discussion of this strategy), the training and validation losses reflected the inability of our model to predict ICs, even if the model did quickly learn to predict NGSs from ICs (Fig. 2B).
discussion: While we refer to LifeGPT as being "topology-agnostic," our training and testing data only encompass 2 × 2, 4 × 4, 8 × 8, 16 × 16, and 32 × 32 toroidal grids. While our data indicate a high likelihood that LifeGPT's architecture is indeed topology-agnostic (able to learn Life on a variety of grid sizes and boundary conditions), future work is also needed to validate the performance of this type of model on much larger, differently shaped, or higher-dimensional topologies. Additionally, our tests focused solely on the rules of the 2D binary CA system of Life; other CA may be easier or harder to learn depending on the rules. We cannot guarantee that the LifeGPT architecture will perform similarly for learning single-timestep global state transitions across all 2D CA. LifeGPT's ever-increasing likelihood of generating incorrect tokens with increasing sampling temperature suggests a fundamental trade-off between model creativity and accuracy in making deterministic predictions. Our results suggest that LifeGPT approximates the rules but fails in certain cases, potentially because of limitations in modeling such systems due to training data, algorithms, or other aspects of how GPT models are built and trained. Nevertheless, a future version of LifeGPT could be used to simulate CA systems that do not require 100% accurate predictions, or for which "perfect" predictions are impossible, such as in the case of stochastic CA, or modeling real-world mechanisms such as the growth or dynamics of living cells, among others. Another possible avenue of exploration would be to use GPT models to predict coarse-grained CA evolution, as previous work has shown that even highly complex CA often have simpler coarse-grained representations using a renormalization group approach.
conclusion: Overall, we have demonstrated the functionality of LifeGPT, a decoder-only GPT model trained on NGS transition examples from Conway's Game of Life. By allowing LifeGPT to learn the dynamics of Life, we demonstrated, to the best of our knowledge, the first ever transformer model that has been trained to predict NGS transitions in a 2D CA system. This may open up a new frontier for applying large-scale neural models to analyze discrete dynamical systems, investigate fundamental computational limits, and develop inverse-design strategies for rule-based generative processes in the life and physical sciences. Key contributions of this work are: • Topology-agnostic transformer architecture: We demonstrate that a decoder-only GPT model, with minimal adaptation, can accurately learn the Game of Life's local update rules without explicit encoding of the two-dimensional grid topology or imposing periodic boundary conditions. This highlights the model's inherent ability to parse local-neighborhood dynamics solely from the data. - Broad-entropy dataset strategy: By constructing training samples with a wide range of order parameters (from fully alive to fully dead), we show that the model overcomes "state-transition asymmetry," enabling nearly perfect next-state predictions. This approach underscores the importance of training set diversity in capturing rule-based phenomena. - Unified framework for multiple grid sizes: We showed that LifeGPT can learn Life's rules simultaneously on multiple grid dimensions (e.g., 2 × 2, 4 × 4, 8 × 8, and 16 × 16), illustrating the potential of transformer-based models to internalize different topological constraints under one set of learned parameters. - Recursive simulation: We introduce an autoregressive loop, conceptually applying the model's own outputs as next inputs—demonstrating multi-step simulation of Conway's Game of Life. Despite the classical autoregressive nature of this procedure, our explicit framing shows how transformers can handle iterative two-dimensional updates at the grid level beyond token-level recurrences. - Near-perfect generalization in a vast configuration space: The model achieves near-zero single-timestep global state transition inference error on most test configurations (Figs. 2, 3, 4, 6, 7), despite training on only 10,000 examples from an astronomical search space of 2^1024. This underscores the capacity of moderately sized GPT architectures to capture deterministic cellular automaton rules with high fidelity, even when exposed to a minuscule fraction of possible game states. However, multi-step simulations can deviate from ground truth if an early timestep prediction is incorrect, a limitation illustrated by the "glider" and "r-pentomino" tests. - Insights on computational (Ir)reducibility: Our findings speak to the broader question of whether neural networks can circumvent the alleged computational irreducibility of Life. While we do not claim to provide an analytic shortcut, the model's localized rule-learning capability suggests transformer attention might uncover limited "pockets of reducibility" in practice, applying to some but not all ICs. - Pathway to inverse design and rule extraction: We discuss how the approach can be extended to infer or design unknown CA rules, bridging discrete dynamical systems and generative AI. Future models might incorporate internal "world models" of CA rule sets, explicit reasoning steps, and other mechanisms, enabling advanced applications in biological pattern formation, material growth processes, and other systems where local interactions scale to global complexity. LifeGPT represents a step forward in the synthesis of machine learning with ALife, and in the understanding of highly complex systems with emergent properties. The demonstrated capability of LifeGPT (which is orders of magnitude smaller than state-of-the-art GPT models) to learn the state-transition rules governing Life with near-perfect accuracy opens up new opportunities for both understanding and modeling highly systems highly sensitive to initial conditions. Future work could also focus on mitigating the effects of occasional errors. The intrinsic stochasticity of the transformer architecture leaves the door open for building better understandings of probabilistic systems, and of coarse-grained approximations of the real world. LifeGPT's topology-agnostic framework and ability to learn across multiple grid sizes, unlike previous strategies for learning Life and other CA, demonstrate potential for broad applications to nonlinear dynamical systems, independent of spatiotemporal dependencies.
notes: Used 'Discussion' as a separate section, and 'Results' includes combined results and discussion content. 'Conclusion' is derived from the final summary of findings.
